attributes.stem:
A 37-year-old man is admitted to the rehabilitation ward following traumatic amputation of his right forearm in an industrial accident six weeks ago. His right hand was his dominant hand. He has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications other than as-needed pain relief. He works as a carpenter and lives with his partner and two children in a metropolitan area.

He has made good progress with his initial rehabilitation goals and is preparing for discharge. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His surgical wound has healed well, and he has begun basic prosthetic training.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most significant barrier to rehabilitation following discharge?
attributes.explanation:
Upper limb amputation, particularly of the dominant hand, presents unique rehabilitation challenges. The key barriers to successful rehabilitation must be identified and addressed to optimise outcomes.

Loss of dominant hand function represents the most significant barrier to rehabilitation in this case. As a carpenter, the patient's livelihood depends on fine motor skills and manual dexterity. Relearning activities of daily living and work-related tasks with the non-dominant hand or a prosthesis requires extensive training and adaptation. This may necessitate occupational retraining or career change.

Physical access to rehabilitation facilities is typically not a major barrier in metropolitan areas. Most rehabilitation services are accessible, and upper limb amputees are generally mobile and able to travel independently. However, this can be a significant barrier for patients living in rural or remote areas where specialised rehabilitation services may be limited.

Cost of prosthetic maintenance, while important, is usually covered by workers' compensation or insurance in cases of workplace injury. This is unlikely to be the most significant barrier to rehabilitation.

Pain management issues, including phantom limb pain, can be challenging but are usually manageable with appropriate medication and therapy. They rarely present the primary barrier to rehabilitation.

Limited prosthetic options is less relevant today given advances in prosthetic technology. Various options exist, from basic mechanical devices to sophisticated myoelectric prostheses.

Successful rehabilitation requires a comprehensive approach addressing physical, psychological, and vocational aspects. The loss of dominant hand function impacts not only activities of daily living but also employment prospects and quality of life. Psychological support, occupational therapy, and vocational counselling are essential components of the rehabilitation program.
learningPoints: Psychological adjustment and acceptance are critical factors in successful rehabilitation after upper limb amputation.
Occupational retraining may be necessary when dominant hand amputation affects employment capabilities.
Social support networks and access to ongoing rehabilitation services are key determinants of long-term outcomes.
references: Philip BA, Frey SH. Compensatory changes accompanying chronic forced use of the nondominant hand by unilateral amputees. Journal of Neuroscience. 2014 Mar 5;34(10):3622-31. Available online at ; https://www.jneurosci.org/content/jneuro/34/10/3622.full.pdf

Daniele Nico, Elena Daprati, François Rigal, Lawrence Parsons, Angela Sirigu, Left and right hand recognition in upper limb amputees, Brain, Volume 127, Issue 1, January 2004, Pages 120–132. Available online at ; https://academic.oup.com/brain/article-abstract/127/1/120/289178?redirectedFrom=fulltext
